title: PX.2 Tool runtime & orchestration
description:
# PX.2 — Tool runtime & orchestration

## What

The runtime that loads `spec/finance_research_tool_registry.json`, selects tool bundles, enforces audience separation (reader vs analyst), gates tools by cost class, intercepts write-intent tools into pending-action approvals, and maintains the prompt-cache prefix order.

## Why

This is the code path that physically enforces I4 (analyst never sees raw untrusted text) and I7 (approvals). Without it, the invariants exist only in documentation.

## Contract / acceptance

- Registry loader reads `finance_research_tool_registry.json` as the source of truth for bundle membership, audience, `read_only`, `approval_required`, `cost_class`, `freshness_expectation`.
- Bundle selector chooses from the 11 bundles based on pre-resolve classification (P2.2).
- Audience enforcement is **static** where possible (typed tool interfaces distinguish reader vs analyst) and **runtime** where necessary (e.g., reject analyst call with raw-document argument).
- Budget gate limits tool-call count per cost class per turn.
- Write-intent tools (`create_alert`, `create_agent`, `add_to_watchlist`) are intercepted: `approval_required: true` → pending action awaiting user confirmation; `approval_required: false` → may execute but remains a policy boundary.
- Prompt-cache prefix order: `tools → system → bundle policy → response schema → few-shots → thread summary → resolved subjects/period` — user turn is last.

## Depends on

- P0.6 (observability tables for tool_call_logs)

## Downstream consumers

- P2.2 (pre-resolve router consumes bundle selector)
- P2.4 (verifier consults approval states)
- P3.2 (reader-audience tools are enforced here)
- P5.1, P5.4 (approval-sensitive tool flows)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Test audience enforcement with adversarial prompts. An analyst-audience tool accepting a raw_blob_url string is a regression.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/tools/Registry.ts`
- `services/tools/Orchestrator.ts`
- `services/tools/AudienceEnforcer.ts`
- `services/tools/ApprovalInterceptor.ts`
close_reason: Accepted PX.2 after verifying all tool-runtime child beads are closed, services/tools tests cover registry loading, bundle selection, audience enforcement, budget gating, approval interception, prompt templates, and CI now runs the tools test suite.

title: P3.5 Promotion rules for candidate facts
description:
# P3.5 — Promotion rules for candidate facts

## What

Deterministic rules that govern when an extracted candidate fact becomes an authoritative `Fact`. Inputs: source class (filing / press / transcript / news / social / upload), extraction confidence, corroboration by other sources, issuer self-attestation, `Metric` definition. Outputs: a new `Fact` row with `verification_status` (`authoritative | corroborated | candidate | disputed`), or a candidate fact that stays pending.

## Why

Without explicit promotion rules, every extracted number races to become truth. The spec's per-source-class promotion discipline (I3) is how documents stay "evidence not truth."

## Contract / acceptance

Promotion rules by source class (lifted from spec):

| Document kind | Typical outputs | Authoritative fact creation |
|---|---|---|
| Filing / primary issuer disclosure | facts, claims, events | Yes |
| Press release / earnings release | facts, claims, events | Yes, under parser rules |
| Transcript | claims, events, candidate facts | Sometimes, with attribution |
| News article / research note | claims, events, candidate facts, impacts | Usually no |
| Tweet / X / Reddit | claims, sentiment, impact leads | No |
| User upload / memo | user-scoped claims, notes, optional candidate facts | User-scoped only unless validated |

- Normalized statement values from P1.2 land as `Fact` rows when authoritative, as `Computation` rows when derived.
- Facts are immutable except through supersession. Restatements use `facts.supersedes` / `superseded_by` — NEVER overwrite.
- `invalidated_at` marks a fact as no longer applicable without supersession.
- Low-confidence extractions route to reviewer queue (P6.3) rather than auto-promoting.

## Depends on

- P1.2 (normalized statement layer — the canonical value plane promotion targets)
- P3.2 (candidate fact extraction)
- P3.4 (claim corroboration context)

## Downstream consumers

- P1.2b (aggregations read authoritative facts)
- P2.4 (snapshot manifests bind to facts with known verification_status)
- P5.3 (findings reference promoted facts for severity scoring)
- P6.3 (reviewer queue consumes low-confidence rejects)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Do NOT use `facts.scale` as a workaround for unit mismatches — normalize before writing.
- Preserve `definition_version` — if a metric definition changes, old and new values must be distinguishable.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/evidence/promotion/Rules.ts`
- `services/evidence/promotion/Supersession.ts`

title: P2.4 Snapshot assembler & verifier
description:
# P2.4 — Snapshot assembler & verifier

## What

The deterministic stage that runs after the analyst tool loop and before streaming the response: stage the snapshot manifest (collect fact/claim/event/series/source/tool-call refs from the tool log), verify bindings and disclosures, seal the snapshot transactionally, and enforce `allowed_transforms` on subsequent interactions.

## Why

This is where invariants I1 (no displayed number without a backing row), I2 (narrative matches visuals), I5 (answer pinned to a moment), I7 (approvals), and I8 (explicit refresh) are physically enforced. Without a verifier, the model can return block shapes that pass schema validation but reference nonexistent facts — producing confident fabrication.

## Contract / acceptance

**Staging:**
- Assemble `SnapshotManifest` from tool-call logs: `subject_refs`, `fact_refs`, `claim_refs`, `event_refs`, `series_specs`, `source_ids`, `tool_call_ids`, `as_of`, `basis`, `normalization`, `coverage_start`.
- Compute `allowed_transforms` explicitly (ranges, intervals, sort fields, `range_end_max: as_of`).

**Verifier checks (all must pass):**
- Every `data_ref` in blocks resolves to a staged fact/series/bundle.
- Every RichText `ref_segment` resolves to a fact/claim/event id in the manifest.
- All cited `source_refs[]` exist in staged `source_ids`.
- Numeric refs match format/period/unit consistency.
- Required disclosures are present (delayed pricing, filing-time basis, low analyst coverage, candidate vs authoritative, etc.).
- All side-effecting tool invocations have approval states resolved (pending or approved) — nothing executes directly from model output.

**Sealing:**
- Snapshot ID minted atomically with manifest persistence.
- `chat_messages.snapshot_id` written only after successful seal.
- Failed verification writes `verifier_fail_logs` row and fails the turn gracefully.

**Transform enforcement:**
- `POST /v1/snapshots/{id}/transform` with a transform listed in `allowed_transforms` and `range_end <= as_of` → 200 with transformed data.
- Transform requiring fresher data, peer-set change, basis change, or normalization change → 409 with `refresh_required`.
- Clients must not mutate sealed snapshots in any path.

## Depends on

- P2.3 (block registry — knows what to verify)
- PX.2 (tool runtime — provides tool call logs + approval state)
- P1.1b (series identity — what's legal as an in-snapshot transform)

## Downstream consumers

- P2.1 (thread coordinator gates message persistence on seal success)
- P4.3 (shared artifact flow — reuses sealed snapshots)
- PX.3 (frontend renderer — enforces presentation-only interactions inside snapshot scope)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Seal order matters: verify → seal → persist chat_messages → stream block.completed events. Streaming before sealing risks showing an invariant-violating block.
- `allowed_transforms` is explicit manifest state, not a UI guess.
- Transforms are SNAPSHOT-level, not block-level. Block kind alone doesn't determine legality.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/snapshot/Assembler.ts`
- `services/snapshot/Verifier.ts`
- `services/snapshot/Sealer.ts`
- `/v1/snapshots/*` handlers
close_reason: Accepted P2.4 after verifying all six child beads are closed: manifest staging, disclosure policy, verifier, transactional sealing, allowed_transforms legality, and refresh_required envelopes. Verified with services/snapshot npm test (90 pass), services/chat npm test (60 pass), and db npm test (15 pass, 19 Docker skips).

title: P1.1b Adjusted series query & caching semantics
description:
# P1.1b — Adjusted series query & caching semantics

## What

The market-data surface above raw bar retrieval that produces comparison-ready or interactive time series from canonical listing subject input, with explicit basis/normalization binding and cache identity rules that preserve snapshot correctness.

## Why

Without explicit basis/normalization binding, series responses silently mix adjusted and unadjusted data, and cache hits return stale material when inputs shift. That breaks I1 (provenance), I5 (answer pinned to a moment), and I8 (explicit refresh). This bead is the primary cache surface for the whole app.

## Contract / acceptance

- Series queries bind explicit `subject_refs`, `range`, `interval`, `basis`, `normalization` before execution, caching, and snapshot binding.
- Market-series basis is explicit: `unadjusted` | `split_adjusted` | `split_and_div_adjusted`.
- The service must not silently mix bases inside one returned series set, one chart artifact, or one comparison response.
- Multi-subject performance responses share one basis, one normalization, one `as_of`, one requested range even when coverage start differs by subject.
- Series responses expose effective coverage window and any partial/unavailable history explicitly.
- Cache is internal optimization, not a separate source of truth.
- Cache identity: canonical subject set × range × interval × basis × normalization × freshness boundary.
- Cache hit may reuse prior series only when the request preserves subject membership, basis, normalization, and does not require data newer than request or snapshot `as_of`.
- Cache hits and misses return the same observable contract.
- Changing basis/normalization/peer set/freshness = different request (refresh, not cache mutation).
- In-snapshot transforms may reuse cached series only when the requested range/interval is legal under the sealed snapshot's `allowed_transforms`.
- Changing basis or normalization is out-of-snapshot behavior even if compatible cached material exists.

## Depends on

- P1.1 (quote/bar provider abstraction)

## Downstream consumers

- P1.3 (price charts + comparison modules)
- P2.3 (block registry — interactive blocks need stable series identity)
- P2.4 (snapshot assembler — `series_specs` in manifest)
- P4.6 (specialized social/news blocks — trend-style blocks reuse this)
- P6.2 (non-US coverage — explicit coverage rules)
- P6.5 (scale hardening — cache identity audits)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Cache identity keying is invariant-critical. Getting it wrong = silent snapshot violation.
- Expose the coverage window; never backfill missing periods.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/market/SeriesService.ts`
- `services/market/cache/*`
- `/v1/market/series` handler
close_reason: All P1.1b child beads are complete: explicit series query binding, cache identity, in-snapshot transform gate, coverage-window exposure, and FX guardrails; verified with services/market npm test.

title: P0.5 DB bootstrap (schema pack + migrations)
description:
# P0.5 — DB bootstrap (schema pack + migrations)

## What

Apply `spec/finance_research_db_schema.sql` against a PostgreSQL 15+ database. Set up a migration runner, seed scripts for reference data (metrics registry, source providers), enum registry, and a partition/index plan for the evidence plane (`facts`, `claims`, `snapshots`, `tool_call_logs`).

## Why

Every service and feature in P1..P6 assumes these tables exist. The schema encodes the evidence plane's truth-vs-evidence discipline (I1, I3) and the identity layer (issuer/instrument/listing). Getting the schema right here prevents ad-hoc table sprawl later.

## Contract / acceptance

- `spec/finance_research_db_schema.sql` applies cleanly on a fresh Postgres 15+ database.
- Extension `pgcrypto` is installed (for `gen_random_uuid`).
- All enum types in the schema pack are created.
- Seed script populates `metrics` with the core metric registry (common ratios, accounting metrics).
- Migration runner supports forward/backward migrations and is used for every schema change after initial apply.
- Indexes in the schema pack are present (notably `facts_subject_metric_idx`, `facts_metric_period_idx`, `claims_document_idx`, `snapshots_created_idx`).
- Partition strategy documented for `facts` and `tool_call_logs` by time (monthly is a sensible default).

## Depends on

- None (foundation work).

## Downstream consumers

- All P0..P6 services depend on this.

## Considerations / gotchas

- D1 is not a fit for the evidence plane — current D1 limits are 10 GB per database on paid plans. Keep evidence plane on Postgres; D1 may carry app metadata if useful.
- `facts.supersedes` / `superseded_by` are self-referential — restatements form a linked chain. Don't break that by overwriting rows.
- `subject_kind + subject_id` polymorphic references are intentional (per-kind FK would explode the table count). Application code enforces referential integrity.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `spec/finance_research_db_schema.sql` (applied, not modified)
- `db/migrations/0001_init.sql` (wraps the schema pack)
- `db/seed/metrics.sql`
- `db/migrate.ts` (runner)
close_reason: All four subtasks closed: 7.1 applied the normative schema pack with verify CLI and Docker-backed integration tests; 7.2 added the up/down/status migration runner with registry tests; 7.3 seeded the core metrics registry (45 metrics) and minimal source registry (7 deterministic-UUID entries) with idempotency verified via digest comparison; 7.4 documented the partition strategy for facts and tool_call_logs plus runnable example DDL that was executed against Postgres 15. Unblocks fra-6al.3, fra-6al.8, fra-6j0.1, fra-cw0.1, fra-cw0.3.

title: P0.3 Identity & resolver service
description:
# P0.3 — Identity & resolver service

## What

The deterministic boundary that converts lookup input (user text or provider-origin identity records) into canonical finance identity outputs: `issuer`, `instrument`, `listing`, or a typed `SubjectRef`. The resolver owns normalization, candidate generation, canonical selection when unambiguous, and explicit ambiguity preservation when multiple canonical targets remain plausible.

## Why

Ticker is a listing locator, not canonical identity. The same symbol can map to different listings, venues, share classes, or ADRs. Without the resolver as a hard boundary, the rest of the system accumulates ticker-string shortcuts that break on dual-class stocks, cross-listings, and restructurings.

## Contract / acceptance

Resolution envelope has three outcomes:

- `resolved` — one canonical target confidently enough for deterministic downstream use.
- `ambiguous` — multiple canonical issuer/instrument/listing/SubjectRef targets remain plausible; returns ranked candidates without silently picking one.
- `not_found` — input normalizable but not mappable to a supported canonical target.

Rules:

- Input families: user-entered lookup text (ticker-like strings, issuer names, aliases) and provider-origin identity records (ticker, exchange, CIK, ISIN, other id-bearing fields).
- Normalization must not erase identity-level distinctions between issuer, instrument, and listing.
- The resolver may start from ticker/alias lookup but must promote the lookup into explicit issuer/instrument/listing/SubjectRef output.
- Ranked candidates are advisory metadata, not permission to silently collapse ambiguity.
- `resolved` responses include the canonical identity level that was chosen.

## Depends on

- P0.5 DB bootstrap (tables `issuers`, `instruments`, `listings`, `themes`).

## Downstream consumers

- P0.3b (search-to-subject flow)
- P1.1 (market data) — listing-appropriate subject handoff, never ticker string
- P1.2 (fundamentals) — issuer-appropriate subject handoff
- P2.2 (pre-resolve router) — consumes resolver envelopes
- P4.1 (themes) and all screener/watchlist/portfolio handoffs

## Considerations / gotchas

- Maintain the three outcomes (`resolved | ambiguous | not_found`) as a strict contract. Silent winner-picking here corrupts every downstream invariant.
- Ambiguity axis matters: "issuer-vs-listing" and "multiple listings for one ticker" are different ambiguity modes that the API should surface when detectable.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/resolver/*`
- `/v1/subjects/resolve` handler
- `/v1/subjects/hydrate` handler
- DB indexes: `issuers_cik_idx`, `issuers_lei_idx`, `instruments_isin_idx`, `listings_ticker_idx`
close_reason: Completed resolver service scope: envelope, normalization, identifier/ticker/CIK/ISIN entry points, numeric ticker fallback, and /v1/subjects/resolve handler are implemented and verified. Remaining alias expansion and indexed alias lookup hardening are tracked separately under fra-6al.4 follow-up beads.

title: Finance Research App — context anchor (vision, invariants, identity rules)
description:
# Finance Research App — context anchor

This bead is a **pinned reference**, not work. It captures the load-bearing design rules that every other bead assumes. When a future session loses context, start here.

## Vision (in one sentence)

> **A finance research app like the one in the reference videos is not "a chatbot with charts." It is an evidence system plus a structured artifact renderer plus a tool-using analyst.**

If you find yourself implementing something that contradicts that sentence (e.g., letting the model emit raw HTML, letting the analyst see untrusted text, letting a displayed number exist without a backing Fact), stop and reconsider.

## Product target

Desktop-first research terminal with five primary workspaces — **Home, Agents, Chat, Screener, Analyze** — plus an entered **Symbol detail** surface family (overview, financials, earnings, holders, signals). The UX expectations lifted from the videos:

- ticker chats and theme/macro chats in the same right-rail thread list
- interactive chart blocks that stay interactive inside old assistant messages
- a structured Analyze memo with editable instructions and source-selection controls
- "Add results to chat"
- a right-rail activity stream showing `Reading` / `Investigating` / `Found` stages
- a Home surface that is **findings-first**, not a raw news page
- light/dark parity; desktop/Electron feel with Expo-friendly later

## Three systems on one data plane

1. **Deterministic terminal surfaces** — watchlists, quotes, charts, symbol tabs, screener, portfolio overlay.
2. **Interactive research chat** — tool-using analyst, structured Block[] responses, persistent threads, cross-thread artifacts.
3. **Background thesis agents** — scheduled research, claim/event monitoring, finding generation, Home feed, right-rail activity, notifications.

These share one evidence plane underneath (facts, claims, events, impacts, snapshots).

## Invariants (I1–I8) — non-negotiable

**I1. No displayed number without a backing row.** Every displayed number must trace to a `Fact` or `Computation` row with provenance, timestamps, and method. The model never invents a number.

**I2. Narrative and visuals cannot disagree.** Prose references facts and claims by ID; values resolve at render time from the same snapshot used by charts and tables.

**I3. Documents are evidence, not truth.** Articles, transcripts, tweets, Reddit threads, uploads are ingested as `Document` objects. They may produce claims/events/candidate-facts. They do not become canonical facts merely by being ingested.

**I4. The analyst never sees raw untrusted text.** Raw external content is processed by the reader path. The analyst consumes only structured outputs. This is the prompt-injection containment boundary.

**I5. Every answer is pinned to a moment.** A response is bound to an immutable `SnapshotManifest`. Interactivity inside the response is allowed only within the snapshot contract.

**I6. Multi-entity reasoning happens through roles and impacts.** A document can mention many subjects, but the reasoning unit is the `Claim` plus its `ClaimArgument`s and `EntityImpact` edges.

**I7. Side effects require approval.** Alerts, exports, emails, private-tool reads, and any transactional tools require explicit user confirmation. Approval-sensitive tools today: `create_alert`, `create_agent`. `add_to_watchlist` is write-intent but can be auto-approved by policy.

**I8. Refresh is explicit.** Interactivity inside a sealed snapshot is allowed only for transforms listed in `allowed_transforms`. Anything requiring fresher data or a changed subject set triggers refresh.

## Identity layer (the most common source of bugs)

- **Issuer** = legal reporting entity (`cik`, `lei`, legal_name).
- **Instrument** = tradable security definition (`isin`, `figi_composite`, `asset_type`, `share_class`).
- **Listing** = venue-specific symbol (`mic`, `ticker`, `trading_currency`, `timezone`).
- **Ticker is a listing locator, not canonical identity.** Same ticker can map to different listings, venues, share classes, ADRs. Never persist or join on ticker where issuer/instrument/listing identity exists.
- `SubjectRef` discriminates: `issuer | instrument | listing | theme | macro_topic | portfolio | screen`.

## Evidence boundary

```
Document  ->  Claim (+ arguments, + impacts)  ->  Event
                 \
                  -> Candidate Fact  ->  (promotion rule)  ->  Fact
```

- Fundamentals consumes `Metric`, `Fact`, `Computation` as the canonical value layer.
- Evidence extraction owns `Source`, `Document`, `Mention`, `Claim`, `ClaimArgument`, `ClaimEvidence`, `ClaimCluster`, `Event`, `EntityImpact`.
- Home feed consumes `Finding`, `SnapshotManifest`, `ClaimCluster` as the deduped product artifact layer.
- Promotion rules by source class:
  - Filing / primary issuer disclosure → may create authoritative facts
  - Earnings transcript → claims and events; facts only via validated extraction
  - News / research notes → claims, events, candidate facts, impacts; rarely authoritative
  - Tweet / X / Reddit → claims, sentiment, leads only
  - User upload → user-scoped evidence unless promoted

## Snapshot contract

```
SnapshotManifest {
  snapshot_id, subject_refs[], as_of, basis, normalization,
  coverage_start, source_ids[], fact_refs[], claim_refs[], event_refs[],
  allowed_transforms: { ranges, intervals, sort_fields, range_end_max: "as_of" }
}
```

- In-snapshot transform: allowed if same subject set, same basis, same normalization, range end ≤ `as_of`, transform listed in `allowed_transforms`.
- Out-of-snapshot: fresher data needed, peer set changes, basis changes, normalization changes, source policy changes → **refresh** (new snapshot).
- This is how historical charts in old messages stay interactive while "answer pinned to a moment" holds.

## Tool architecture (audience boundary)

- **Reader tools** may access raw untrusted text: `search_raw_documents`, `fetch_raw_document`, `extract_mentions`, `extract_claims`, `extract_candidate_facts`, `extract_events`, `classify_sentiment`.
- **Analyst tools** operate only on structured outputs. Raw text must not leak.
- The system selects the bundle; the model chooses tools within the bundle.
- Write-intent tools (`create_alert`, `create_agent`, `add_to_watchlist`) remain policy boundaries even when auto-approved.
- Normative registry: `spec/finance_research_tool_registry.json`.

## Block model (assistant output contract)

- Assistant output is always a typed `Block[]`, not markdown and not a tool call.
- Every block inherits `BaseBlock`: `id`, `kind`, `snapshot_id`, `data_ref`, `source_refs[]`, `as_of`.
- Block kinds: `rich_text`, `section`, `metric_row`, `table`, `line_chart`, `revenue_bars`, `perf_comparison`, `segment_donut`, `segment_trajectory`, `metrics_comparison`, `analyst_consensus`, `price_target_range`, `eps_surprise`, `filings_list`, `news_cluster`, `finding_card`, `sentiment_trend`, `mention_volume`, `sources`, `disclosure`.
- `Sources` is required whenever external evidence was used.
- `Disclosure` may be injected by orchestration.
- Chat, Analyze, and agent-produced findings all render through the same `BlockRegistry`.
- Normative schema: `spec/finance_research_block_schema.json`.

## Deliberate non-goals

- live market streaming inside sealed assistant messages
- raw web access by the analyst
- brokerage execution or order management
- implicit trading workflows
- exposing chain-of-thought
- using document mentions as a proxy for entity impact
- treating tweets or Reddit posts as authoritative facts
- making MCP the canonical internal architecture (MCP is optional interop, not the core)

## Normative artifacts

- `stock-agent-v2.md` — consolidated architecture plan
- `spec/finance_research_spec.md` — implementation contract (source of P-codes)
- `spec/finance_research_db_schema.sql` — relational schema pack
- `spec/finance_research_openapi.yaml` — HTTP API contracts
- `spec/finance_research_block_schema.json` — Block[] schema
- `spec/finance_research_tool_registry.json` — tool bundle registry
- `vid1.mp4`, `vid2.mp4` — reference UX

## How to use this graph

1. `bd ready` to see unblocked work.
2. `bd show <id>` for a full briefing on a specific task.
3. `bd dep tree <id>` to see what unblocks when you close something.
4. `bd list --labels phase:P1` to scope to one phase.
5. `bd list --labels surface:chat` to scope to one surface.
6. `bd list --labels invariant:I4` to audit prompt-injection-boundary guardians.
close_reason: Reference bead - always pinned, never work

title: Handle numeric ticker inputs before CIK fallback in resolver
description: Digit-only free-text inputs like '700' currently take the identifier_hint path as CIKs and never reach listing ticker resolution. The resolver should either try ticker resolution before returning unknown_cik for numeric input, or otherwise preserve valid numeric listing lookups while keeping CIK behavior deterministic.
close_reason: Identifier-shaped input now falls back to ticker resolution when the identifier miss returns not_found, covering digit-only numeric tickers as well as other dual-signal inputs.

title: PX.1 Observability (tool_call_log, citation_log, verifier_fail, evals)
description:
# PX.1 — Observability (tool_call_log, citation_log, verifier_fail, evals)

## What

The observability and evaluation surface: structured logging (`tool_call_logs`, `citation_logs`, `verifier_fail_logs`), extraction eval metrics, golden-set evaluation runs, drift report generation.

## Why

Without observability, invariant regressions are invisible until users notice. The spec names these log tables as day-one essentials — not an afterthought — because evals are how we catch drift before it ships.

## Contract / acceptance

- Every tool invocation writes `tool_call_logs` with duration, status, error_code.
- Every block citation writes `citation_logs` bound to `snapshot_id`.
- Verifier failures write `verifier_fail_logs` with `reason_code` and `details`.
- Extraction evals run nightly against a golden set; results land in `eval_run_results`.
- Golden set categories (from spec): ticker/name disambiguation; issuer-vs-listing confusion; themes and macro topics; fiscal/calendar alignment; corporate actions; restatements; segment redefinitions; low analyst coverage; delisted/acquired names; multi-entity documents; candidate-vs-authoritative fact promotion; social rumor handling; block-choice correctness; snapshot transform correctness.
- Drift report flags regressions between consecutive eval runs.

## Depends on

- P0.6 (log tables bootstrapped)

## Downstream consumers

- All phases (observability is consumed universally).

## Considerations / gotchas

- Eval runs are not free. Schedule nightly, not per-PR.
- Track `model_version` and `prompt_version` so eval results are attributable.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `observability/logger/*`
- `evals/golden/*`
- `evals/runner.ts`
close_reason: Accepted PX.1 after verifying all observability child beads are closed and services/observability tests cover tool_call_logs, citation_logs, verifier_fail_logs, eval_run_results, golden evals, drift reports, and agent_run_logs; db tests also pass locally with Docker-only cases skipped.

title: PX.3 Frontend renderer & BlockRegistry
description:
# PX.3 — Frontend renderer & BlockRegistry

## What

The React-side rendering layer: `BlockRegistry` keyed by block `kind`, streaming block rendering driven by SSE events, virtualized message list for long threads, memoized blocks by content hash, skeleton-first chart rendering, scroll tailing with jump-to-latest.

## Why

Block rendering is the user-visible surface. Performance cliffs here (long threads re-render, charts flash, scroll jumps) kill the feel of the videos.

## Contract / acceptance

- `BlockRegistry` is the single source for `kind → Component` mapping; chat, Analyze, and findings share it.
- SSE-driven incremental rendering: `block.began` creates a skeleton; `block.delta` updates; `block.completed` finalizes.
- Virtualized message list in long threads (windowing).
- Blocks memoized by content hash so unchanged blocks never re-render.
- Charts render skeleton first, data after.
- Scroll tails new messages; jump-to-latest affordance when user has scrolled up.
- Chart blocks fetch data via backend (`snapshot_id + transform`) — NEVER direct vendor calls.
- Interactivity stays inside snapshot scope unless user explicitly refreshes.

## Depends on

- P2.1 (SSE transport)
- P2.3 (block registry + renderers)
- P2.4 (snapshot transform endpoint)

## Downstream consumers

- (leaf — user-visible)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Frontend renderer must never treat rendering as a tool invocation. Output is typed data; rendering is typed UI.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `app/chat/ChatView.tsx`
- `app/chat/MessageList.tsx` (virtualized)
- `app/blocks/Registry.ts`
close_reason: Closed

title: P5.3 Finding generation & severity scoring
description:
# P5.3 — Finding generation & severity scoring

## What

The service that produces `Finding` objects during agent runs: sealed snapshot per finding, subject refs, claim_cluster_ids, severity (`low | medium | high | critical`), headline, and `summary_blocks: Block[]` rendering via the same `BlockRegistry`.

## Why

Findings are the durable product artifact of agents. They live on Home, can be added to chat (P4.3), can trigger alerts (P5.4), and must reference sealed snapshots so their content is reproducible.

## Contract / acceptance

- `Finding { finding_id, agent_id, snapshot_id, subject_refs[], claim_cluster_ids[], severity, headline, summary_blocks[], created_at }`.
- Every finding references a sealed snapshot (finding generation calls P2.4).
- Severity scoring combines evidence strength (trust tier, corroboration count), impact (direction × channel × horizon), and thesis relevance.
- `summary_blocks` validates against the block schema — just like chat/Analyze.
- Findings dedupe on Home via `ClaimCluster`.

## Depends on

- P2.4 (snapshot assembler)
- P3.4 (claim clusters)

## Downstream consumers

- P4.4 (Home feed)
- P5.4 (alert evaluation)
- P5.5 (notification composition)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Severity thresholds should be tuned against the golden-eval suite (PX.1), not set arbitrarily.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/agents/FindingGenerator.ts`
- `services/agents/SeverityScorer.ts`

title: P5.4 Alert evaluation & approval gating
description:
# P5.4 — Alert evaluation & approval gating

## What

Compiles per-agent `alert_rules[]` to evaluable predicates, evaluates them against findings + facts + claims + events per run, and enforces approval gating for `create_alert` (approval-sensitive tool).

## Why

Alerts are side-effecting (they send notifications). Without approval gating, the analyst could create alerts silently. The rule-compile layer also ensures alert rules are declarative rather than opaque code paths — reviewable by the user.

## Contract / acceptance

- Alert rules are declarative structures (subject scope, condition predicates, channels).
- Rule evaluation is deterministic and explainable — each firing points to the claim/fact/event/finding that triggered it.
- `create_alert` returns `pending_action_id` until user confirms.
- Rule firing writes an `alerts_fired` log row; notification composition is P5.5.

## Depends on

- P5.1 (agent alert_rules config)
- P5.2 (evaluation runs inside the agent loop)
- P5.3 (findings are inputs)
- PX.2 (approval-sensitive tool flow)

## Downstream consumers

- P5.5 (notifications consume fired alerts)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Allow dry-run: a user creating a rule can test it against historical data before enabling.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/agents/AlertEvaluator.ts`
- `services/agents/AlertRuleCompiler.ts`

title: P5.5 Notification channels (push/email/SMS/digest)
description:
# P5.5 — Notification channels (push/email/SMS/digest)

## What

Notification delivery across web push, email, SMS, mobile push, and scheduled digests. Channels honor per-fact `entitlement_channels[]` — some facts are `app`-only and may not egress via email or push.

## Why

Notifications move content outside the app boundary. `entitlement_channels` is how license/compliance controls propagate to egress. Without this gate, a delayed-pricing fact could be emailed in a way that violates the data provider's license.

## Contract / acceptance

- Channels: web push, email, SMS, mobile push, digest.
- Every outbound notification checks `entitlement_channels[]` on referenced facts; `app`-only facts cannot be rendered in notifications that egress.
- Digests batch multiple findings per channel; respect user preference on cadence.
- User can configure channels per-agent and globally.

## Depends on

- P5.4 (fired alerts are inputs)

## Downstream consumers

- (leaf — egress boundary)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Throttle aggressively. A burst of agents firing simultaneously must not produce hundreds of notifications.
- SMS is expensive — default off, opt-in only.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/notifications/Channels/*`
- `services/notifications/DigestScheduler.ts`

title: P5.1 Agent CRUD, scheduling, watermark storage
description:
# P5.1 — Agent CRUD, scheduling, watermark storage

## What

User-owned background research agents: CRUD, thesis/universe/cadence/alert-rules/prompt-template config, transactional watermark storage that tracks "what have we already processed" for each agent, and the approval flow for `create_agent` (approval-sensitive tool).

## Why

Agents are the engine behind Home. Without watermark discipline, re-runs either duplicate work or miss deltas. Without approval gating on `create_agent`, the analyst could spawn agents silently — an invariant I7 violation.

## Contract / acceptance

- `Agent { agent_id, user_id, name, thesis, universe (SubjectRef[] | dynamic: screen|theme|portfolio), source_policy, cadence, prompt_template, alert_rules[], watermarks, enabled }`.
- Watermarks advance **transactionally** after each successful run — never before.
- `create_agent` is approval-sensitive (per tool registry) — returns `pending_action_id` until user confirms.
- Universe may reference a dynamic watchlist (P4.7) — regenerated per run.
- Alert rules compile to evaluable predicates over Facts, Claims, Events, Impacts.
- `cadence` is human-readable (`hourly | daily | on-filing | ...`) and compiled to a schedule.

## Depends on

- P0.4b (baseline user-owned list model)
- PX.2 (approval-sensitive tool flow)

## Downstream consumers

- P5.2 (queue runner reads due agents)
- P5.3 (findings tied to agent runs)
- P5.4 (alert rules evaluated per run)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Watermarks are JSON by shape — make them versioned so schema evolution doesn't re-process history.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/agents/AgentRepo.ts`
- `services/agents/AlertRuleCompiler.ts`
- `/v1/agents/*` handlers

title: P5.2 Queue runner & idempotent agent runs
description:
# P5.2 — Queue runner & idempotent agent runs

## What

Background runner that consumes scheduled agent due-events from a queue (Cloudflare Queues or equivalent), runs the agent loop (ingest deltas → cluster → score relevance → analyst run → emit findings + activity → evaluate alerts → advance watermark), and does all of this **idempotently**.

## Why

Cloudflare Queues provide at-least-once delivery and do NOT guarantee publish order. Re-processing a message must not duplicate findings or double-advance watermarks. This is a hard constraint.

## Contract / acceptance

- Queue handler accepts an agent-run message with agent_id + run_id.
- Duplicate run_id is detected and short-circuits with no side effects.
- Agent loop: load due agent → read deltas since watermark → reader extraction → cluster/corroborate/update impacts → score relevance to thesis → analyst on structured evidence → emit findings → emit activities → evaluate alerts → advance watermark transactionally.
- Watermark advance and finding emission share one transaction.
- Per-agent concurrency cap (default 1) prevents parallel runs for the same agent.

## Depends on

- P5.1 (agent model + watermarks)
- P3.2 (ingest pipeline)
- P3.4 (clustering)
- P2.4 (snapshot assembler — findings are sealed)

## Downstream consumers

- P5.3 (findings)
- P5.4 (alerts)
- P5.5 (notifications)
- P4.5 (activity stream)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Do not assume FIFO ordering on queue messages. Out-of-order deliveries must converge to the right state.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/agents/QueueRunner.ts`
- `services/agents/AgentLoop.ts`

title: P4.7 Dynamic watchlists & portfolio overlays
description:
# P4.7 — Dynamic watchlists & portfolio overlays

## What

Watchlist derivation modes beyond manual: `screen`, `agent`, `theme`, `portfolio`. Plus portfolio overlays across watchlists, themes, screens, and subject views — driven by P1.5's overlay input contract.

## Why

The videos show watchlists that auto-populate from screens, themes, and portfolios. The derivation modes must reuse replayable definitions (not frozen row caches) to stay accurate. Portfolio overlays need to layer held-state on any subject-collection view without rewriting base surfaces.

## Contract / acceptance

- `Watchlist.mode ∈ { manual, screen, agent, theme, portfolio }`.
- `screen` mode regenerates membership from the saved screen definition — no frozen rows.
- `agent` mode mirrors the agent's universe.
- `theme` mode mirrors `ThemeMembership` for a theme.
- `portfolio` mode mirrors a portfolio's holdings.
- Portfolio overlays use the overlay input contract (P1.5) — read models keyed by subject × contributing portfolio.
- Overlays merge portfolio context with watchlists, themes, screens, and subject views consistently.
- Multiple portfolios with different base currencies stay distinct (no silent FX netting).
- Users can see both watchlist and held state on the same row without collapsing concepts.

## Depends on

- P0.4b (manual baseline)
- P1.5b (shared surface behaviors)
- P1.4b (saved screens as replayable sources)

## Downstream consumers

- P5.1 (agents can target dynamic watchlists as universe)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Derivation modes recompute membership — make cost obvious to users (e.g., a screen-derived list with 500 members is expensive to refresh).

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/watchlists/DynamicMembership.ts`
- `services/overlays/OverlayComposer.ts`
- `app/watchlists/DynamicWatchlist.tsx`

title: P4.4 Home feed ranking & cluster dedupe
description:
# P4.4 — Home feed ranking & cluster dedupe

## What

The cross-agent findings surface: aggregates `Finding` objects across active agents, dedupes by `ClaimCluster`, groups by theme/subject where appropriate, ranks by recency + severity + user relevance, collapses to headline + source + subject/theme tag + timestamp. Plus market pulse, watchlist movers, agent summaries, pinned screens.

## Why

The videos' Home tab is findings-first, not a raw news page. The three hard parts are: cluster dedupe (one story in three outlets = one card), ranking (recency + severity + relevance), and not becoming a raw newsfeed.

## Contract / acceptance

- Query findings across all active agents for the user.
- Dedupe on `ClaimCluster` — one card per cluster, support_count shown.
- Group by theme/subject where appropriate.
- Rank: `score = w_recency * recency + w_severity * severity + w_relevance * user_affinity` (weights configurable).
- Collapsed card: headline + primary source + subject/theme tag + timestamp + severity badge.
- Expandable detail surfaces `summary_blocks` (Block[]) from the finding.
- Secondary sections: market pulse, watchlist movers, agent summaries, pinned screens.
- Findings deep-link into the right deterministic surface — symbol detail, theme thread, or Analyze — rather than inventing custom readouts per card.
- Home renders via the same `BlockRegistry` — `finding_card` block, not custom HTML.

## Depends on

- P1.3 (stable symbol-tab destinations for deep-links)
- P3.4 (claim clusters)
- P5.3 (findings exist)

## Downstream consumers

- PX.3 (frontend rendering)

## Considerations / gotchas

- User affinity signals come from watchlist membership, portfolio holdings, and thread history — respect privacy.
- Do NOT let a high-volume low-severity cluster (e.g., routine analyst upgrades) crowd out a critical finding.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/home/FeedService.ts`
- `services/home/Ranker.ts`
- `/v1/home/*` handlers
- `app/home/Home.tsx`

title: P4.5 Right-rail activity stream
description:
# P4.5 — Right-rail activity stream

## What

The shell-owned right-rail surface for live agent execution telemetry: `Reading`, `Investigating`, `Found`, `Dismissed` stages. Distinct from findings — activity is execution telemetry, findings are durable product artifacts.

## Why

The videos show the right-rail filling with live activity as agents work: "Reading 10-Q for AAPL", "Investigating claim cluster X", "Found: guidance update". Keeping this distinct from findings prevents the user from conflating transient telemetry with durable research output.

## Contract / acceptance

- `RunActivity { run_activity_id, agent_id, stage: reading|investigating|found|dismissed, subject_refs[], source_refs[], summary, ts }`.
- Right-rail renders a reverse-chronological stream of recent activities.
- Activities emit in near-real-time via SSE while an agent run is active; persist to table for later scrollback.
- Activities are NOT findings. `Finding` is durable product; `RunActivity` is execution state.
- Dismissed activities indicate agent evaluated and rejected — they still emit to the stream for transparency.
- The right-rail slot is shell-owned (P0.1); surfaces opt in.

## Depends on

- P0.1 (shell-owned right-rail slot)
- P5.2 (queue runner emits RunActivity during runs)

## Downstream consumers

- (leaf)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Don't flood the stream — rate-limit per-agent to prevent one noisy agent from drowning others.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `app/shell/RightRail.tsx`
- `app/agents/ActivityStream.tsx`
- `services/agents/ActivityEmitter.ts`

title: P4.6 Specialized social & news blocks
description:
# P4.6 — Specialized social & news blocks

## What

Interactive, evidence-bound behavior for block kinds that surface social and news content: `news_cluster`, `sentiment_trend`, `mention_volume`. These kinds are defined in P2.3's initial block catalog; P4.6 implements their **domain-specific behavior** when rendered inside the `signals` symbol tab or elsewhere in social/news contexts.

**Scope distinction vs P2.3:**
- P2.3 defines the block **schema** and implements **basic rendering shells** for all 20 block kinds.
- P4.6 implements the **specialized interactive behavior**: click-through to ClaimCluster detail, sentiment aggregation over time, mention-volume series with cache-safe transforms, and the composition patterns that let `signals` render evidence-backed content without raw social panes.

## Why

The videos show Reddit + news pulse as first-class symbol content, but rendering them as raw text panes would break I4 (analyst never sees raw text; renderer shows evidence-backed structure). P4.6 makes sure these blocks always compose clusters, trend series, and cited evidence — never free-form social-feed scrolls.

## Contract / acceptance

- `news_cluster { cluster_id, headline, claim_refs[], document_refs[] }` — bound to a `ClaimCluster` with supporting documents; click expands to evidence bundle.
- `sentiment_trend { series[] }` — aggregated sentiment over time for a subject or cluster.
- `mention_volume { series[] }` — raw mention counts (prose must include disclosure: mentions ≠ impact).
- All three reuse the same series semantics + cache contract as P1.1b trend-style blocks.
- `signals` tab composes these evidence-backed blocks — NOT raw social-text panes or provider-specific mini-pages.

## Depends on

- P2.3 (block schema + basic renderer shells)
- P1.1b (trend series semantics + cache identity rules)
- P1.2b (key stats + consensus aggregate envelopes for narrative)
- P3.4 (claim clusters)
- P3.6 (evidence bundles for click-through)

## Downstream consumers

- (leaf — these are rendered blocks)

## Considerations / gotchas

- `mention_volume` is easy to misinterpret as "popularity" — include disclosure about the distinction from impact.
- Sentiment is inherently noisy from social sources — expose confidence + source composition.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `app/blocks/NewsCluster.tsx` (specialized behavior layer)
- `app/blocks/SentimentTrend.tsx`
- `app/blocks/MentionVolume.tsx`
- `app/symbol/signals/SignalsTab.tsx` (composition)

title: P4.1 Themes & macro subjects
description:
# P4.1 — Themes & macro subjects

## What

First-class research subjects beyond issuer/instrument/listing: `Theme`, `ThemeMembership`, `macro_topic`. Themes support manual, rule-based, or inferred membership. Theme chats ("Iran-US Trade Tensions", "AI Chips Alpha") sit in the same right-rail thread list as ticker chats.

## Why

The videos show theme/macro chats as equal peers to ticker chats. Without themes as first-class subjects, users either jam theme research into ticker chats (losing subject discipline) or the app grows a separate "theme" UI track that reinvents subject hydration, snapshotting, and agent universes.

## Contract / acceptance

- `Theme { theme_id, name, description, membership_mode: manual|rule_based|inferred, membership_spec?, active_from, active_to? }`
- `ThemeMembership { theme_id, subject_ref, score, rationale_claim_ids[], effective_at, expires_at? }`
- Themes and macro topics are valid `SubjectRef.kind` values (`theme`, `macro_topic`).
- Theme-derived universes may come from screen handoff (P1.4b) — screen-to-theme handoff carries explicit `screen` source reference so membership provenance is explainable.
- Inferred membership uses `ClaimCluster` + `EntityImpact` signals.
- Chat threads with `primary_subject_kind = theme` work end-to-end: pre-resolve, analyst bundle `theme_research`, snapshot, block render — no special-cased code paths.

## Depends on

- P0.3 (resolver extends to themes)
- P1.4b (screen-to-theme handoff)

## Downstream consumers

- P4.4 (Home groups findings by theme tag)
- P4.7 (dynamic watchlists — theme mode)
- P5.1 (agents can scope universe to a theme)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Inferred membership must rebuild safely — explain via `rationale_claim_ids[]` rather than opaque scores.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/themes/ThemeService.ts`
- `services/themes/MembershipEngine.ts`
close_reason: All children shipped: fra-2ju (Theme/ThemeMembership models), fra-95e (bundle routing), fra-vme (inferred membership). Coordinator wired in 2f03e18 — chooseBundleIdForSubjectKind reaches the runner via ChatTurnRunContext.bundleId; SSE consumers see bundle_id on turn.started/turn.completed. 126 chat tests pass.

title: P4.2 Analyze template system
description:
# P4.2 — Analyze template system

## What

Saved, template-driven analysis workflow with editable instructions, selectable source categories, optional added peer/benchmark subjects, a structured memo layout, reruns, and "Add results to chat". Renders via the same `BlockRegistry` as chat.

## Why

Analyze templates encode recurring research patterns (e.g., "Quarterly earnings memo", "Competitive snapshot", "Thesis review"). The videos show Analyze as a distinct top-level workspace that produces memo-style artifacts — not "chat with a symbol loaded."

## Contract / acceptance

- `AnalyzeTemplate { template_id, user_id, name, prompt_template, source_categories[], added_subject_refs[], block_layout_hint?, peer_policy?, disclosure_policy?, version }`.
- Users select **source categories** (company profile, financials annual/quarterly, news, reddit, etc.) and **layout** — NOT raw internal tool names. Orchestrator maps categories to safe bundles + tool policies.
- Analyze runs via the `analyze_template_run` bundle (PX.2 tool runtime).
- Structured memo renders through `BlockRegistry`: `section` containers at the top level, nested chart/table/rich_text/sources blocks.
- Reruns produce a new sealed snapshot — old memo stays readable at its original snapshot.
- "Add results to chat" hands off the sealed snapshot into a chat thread with shared provenance (reuse, not re-run).

## Depends on

- P1.3b (symbol → Analyze handoff)
- P2.3 (block registry)
- P2.4 (snapshot assembler — memos are sealed artifacts)

## Downstream consumers

- P4.3 (shared artifact flow — Analyze memos added to chat)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Do NOT expose raw internal tool names to users. Keep UI vocabulary at "source categories" + "layout hints".
- Peer policy controls whether added subjects are benchmarks, peers, or out-of-universe — different semantics for comparison blocks.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/analyze/TemplateRepo.ts`
- `services/analyze/TemplateRunner.ts`
- `app/analyze/*`

title: P4.3 Shared artifact flow (add-to-chat)
description:
# P4.3 — Shared artifact flow (add-to-chat)

## What

The cross-surface handoff that lets an Analyze memo (or a finding, or a block) be added to a chat thread as a referenced artifact — sharing the sealed snapshot rather than re-running the analyst. Keeps provenance coherent across Home, Analyze, and Chat.

## Why

The videos show "Add results to chat" as a first-class gesture. If adding were a re-run, users would get inconsistent numbers between the memo and the added chat message. If it were a screenshot, interactivity would break. Sharing the sealed snapshot preserves both interactivity and I2 (narrative matches visuals across surfaces).

## Contract / acceptance

- An Analyze memo, a Finding, or a selected block can be "added to chat".
- Added artifacts carry their original `snapshot_id` — chat messages with added artifacts reference the same sealed manifest.
- In-snapshot transforms on added artifacts work inside the chat message (e.g., range change on a `perf_comparison` block).
- Out-of-snapshot interactions produce an explicit refresh prompt (not a silent refetch).
- `chat_messages.blocks` may include artifact-reference blocks pointing to the shared snapshot.
- The chat thread's `latest_snapshot_id` advances only when the chat itself produces a new snapshot — adding an artifact does NOT overwrite thread snapshot.

## Depends on

- P2.4 (sealed snapshots are the transfer unit)
- P1.3b (Analyze entry handoff pattern)

## Downstream consumers

- P6.4 (export/share policy decides what private overlay state travels with shared artifacts)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Entitlement channels: a fact may be `app`-only but not `export` — shared artifacts must respect this at handoff.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/artifact/SharedArtifact.ts`
- `app/chat/ArtifactReferenceBlock.tsx`
close_reason: Closed

title: P3.6 Evidence-bundle assembler
description:
# P3.6 — Evidence-bundle assembler

## What

The service that builds `EvidenceBundle` objects on request: given a set of `claim_ids` or `event_ids`, return the canonical set of supporting documents, evidence locators (`claim_evidence.locator`), and evidence excerpts needed to justify a claim. Implements analyst-facing `build_evidence_bundle` and `get_evidence_bundle` tools.

## Why

The analyst never sees raw document bodies (I4). When it needs to justify a claim in its response (e.g., "revenue grew 12% YoY driven by Services, per the Q3 10-Q"), it calls `get_evidence_bundle` and receives structured evidence with locators and excerpt hashes — not raw text.

## Contract / acceptance

- `build_evidence_bundle({claim_ids?, event_ids?})` returns `{bundle_id, documents[], evidence[]}`.
- Evidence entries carry `{claim_id, document_id, locator (json), excerpt_hash, confidence}`.
- Documents in the bundle expose metadata (`title`, `author`, `published_at`, `canonical_url`, `source.trust_tier`) but NOT raw body.
- Bundles are immutable — a bundle_id always resolves to the same content.
- The analyst-facing response can cite `bundle_id` in block `source_refs[]` and RichText `ref_kind: claim` segments.

## Depends on

- P3.4 (claims + events exist)

## Downstream consumers

- Analyst tool `get_evidence_bundle` (PX.2)
- P4.2 (Analyze memos build bundles for source sections)
- P4.6 (specialized news/social blocks cite bundles)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Excerpt hashes (not raw excerpts) go through analyst-audience tools — this preserves I4 while letting the frontend fetch verified excerpts via a separate reader-audience endpoint.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/evidence/bundle/BundleAssembler.ts`
- `/v1/evidence/evidence-bundles` handler

title: P3.3 Entity linking & mentions
description:
# P3.3 — Entity linking & mentions

## What

Reader-side entity linking that produces `Mention` rows: for every document, identify the subjects (issuers, instruments, listings, themes, macro topics) mentioned, their `prominence` (headline, lead, body, incidental), `mention_count`, and `confidence`.

## Why

A single news article about "Apple's Q3 results" may mention Apple (issuer), AAPL (listing), AAPL options chain (instrument), chips supply theme (theme), and semiconductors (macro topic). Mentions are how reader output fans out to the right set of subjects for downstream routing, but mentions alone are NOT evidence of impact — that's what `EntityImpact` is for (I6).

## Contract / acceptance

- Every ingested `Document` produces zero or more `Mention` rows.
- `mentions.prominence` is one of `headline | lead | body | incidental` — prominence affects later ranking (headline mentions weigh more for Home feed).
- `mentions.confidence` is reader's self-reported entity-linking confidence.
- Mentions reference `subject_kind + subject_id` polymorphically.
- Mentions do NOT imply entity impact — a document can mention a subject incidentally without affecting it. Routing and alerting use `EntityImpact`, not `Mention`.

## Depends on

- P3.1 (documents table)
- P0.3 (resolver — entity linking consults canonical identity)

## Downstream consumers

- P3.4 (claims reference documents; arguments + impacts layer on top of mentions)
- P4.4 (Home feed ranks by mention prominence + severity + recency)
- P4.6 (mention_volume block reads aggregated mentions)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Do NOT use mention counts as a proxy for entity impact. That's a deliberate non-goal called out in the spec.
- Entity linking may be ambiguous — preserve ranked candidates like the resolver does, rather than silently picking.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/evidence/reader/EntityLinker.ts`
- `services/evidence/reader/extract_mentions.ts` (tool wrapper)

title: P3.4 Claim / event / impact graph & claim clustering
description:
# P3.4 — Claim / event / impact graph & claim clustering

## What

The core reasoning graph: `Claim`, `ClaimArgument`, `EntityImpact`, `ClaimEvidence`, `Event`, `EventSubject`, plus `ClaimCluster` / `ClaimClusterMember` for deduping similar claims. A document can fan out into multiple claims, each with different subject roles and impacts. Events aggregate claims and source refs into state-change records.

## Why

The reasoning unit is the claim plus its arguments and impacts — not the document. This is invariant I6: a news article about a regulator fining a supplier can produce one claim with arguments `{regulator: subject, supplier: object, manufacturer: affected_party}` and impacts `{supplier: negative/regulation, manufacturer: negative/supply_chain}`. Clustering dedupes claims across sources so the Home feed shows one card for a story, not three.

## Contract / acceptance

- `Claim` carries `predicate`, `text_canonical`, `polarity`, `modality` (`asserted | estimated | speculative | rumored | quoted`), `attributed_to_type`, `effective_time`, `confidence`, `status` (`extracted | corroborated | disputed | rejected`).
- `ClaimArgument` binds claim to subjects with `role` (`subject | object | customer | supplier | competitor | regulator | beneficiary | constrained_party | affected_party`).
- `EntityImpact` routes claims to subjects with `direction` (`positive | negative | mixed | unknown`), `channel` (`demand | pricing | supply_chain | regulation | competition | balance_sheet | sentiment`), `horizon` (`near_term | medium_term | long_term`).
- `Event` has `event_type` (`earnings_release | guidance_update | rating_change | m_and_a | split | dividend | product_launch | lawsuit | macro_event | theme_event`) + `status` + `source_claim_ids` + `source_ids`.
- `ClaimCluster.canonical_signature` is unique; similar claims across sources join a cluster via `ClaimClusterMember` with `relation: support | contradict`.
- Cluster has `support_count`, `contradiction_count`, `aggregate_confidence` for ranking.

## Depends on

- P3.2 (claims/events come from reader extraction)
- P3.3 (claims arguments reference subjects via mentions)

## Downstream consumers

- P3.5 (promotion rules consult modality + source class)
- P3.6 (evidence bundles package claims + supporting documents)
- P4.4 (Home feed dedupes on ClaimCluster)
- P5.3 (findings reference claim_cluster_ids)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Canonical signature design is critical for cluster quality. Don't hash only the text — include subject set + event type + effective time.
- Impact routing drives alerting. A claim about a supplier should route impacts to the manufacturer by supply-chain channel.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/evidence/graph/ClaimRepo.ts`
- `services/evidence/graph/EventRepo.ts`
- `services/evidence/graph/Clusterer.ts`

title: P3.1 Source & document models + object storage
description:
# P3.1 — Source & document models + object storage

## What

The persistence layer for evidence ingestion: `Source`, `Document`, `Mention` tables already in the schema pack, plus R2/S3 object storage for raw blobs (referenced by `documents.raw_blob_id`), plus content-hash dedupe at the document level.

## Why

Raw document bytes belong outside the relational schema — they are large, immutable, and rarely queried after ingestion. Referencing them by blob id keeps the evidence graph small and makes deletion/retention straightforward. Content-hash dedupe at ingest prevents a single press release retrieved from multiple aggregators from producing multiple rows.

## Contract / acceptance

- Raw blobs land in object storage (R2/S3) keyed by `raw_blob_id` derived from content hash.
- `documents` rows reference `raw_blob_id` and carry `content_hash`, `parse_status`, `provider_doc_id`, `published_at`, `lang`.
- `sources` carry `trust_tier` (`primary | secondary | tertiary | user`) and `license_class`.
- Unique index on `(content_hash, raw_blob_id)` prevents duplicate document rows.
- `parent_document_id` supports threaded sources (Reddit threads, transcripts with segments).
- `deleted_at` supports soft-delete for retention/license compliance.
- Access to raw blobs is read-only; updates are never in-place (superseded documents get new rows).

## Depends on

- P0.5 (schema applied)

## Downstream consumers

- P3.2 (ingestion writes here)
- P3.3 (mentions reference documents)
- P3.4 (claims reference documents + sources)
- P3.6 (evidence bundles package documents)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Licensing: some provider content cannot be stored raw. Support a `license_class` that routes between blob storage and ephemeral fetch.
- Never expose raw blob URLs to analyst-audience tools. Reader tools only.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/evidence/ObjectStore.ts` (R2/S3 adapter)
- `services/evidence/DocumentRepo.ts`
- `services/evidence/SourceRepo.ts`
close_reason: Accepted P3.1 after all four child beads and the S3 wire adapter dependency landed: fra-5gb (Source repo + trust_tier + license_class), fra-131 (Document repo + content-hash dedupe), fra-8la (parent_document_id threading + recursive-CTE traversal helpers), fra-0v4 (content-addressed ObjectStore contract + MemoryObjectStore), fra-ujp (S3ObjectStore wire adapter for R2/S3/MinIO). Schema acceptance: documents reference raw_blob_id as sha256:<64-hex>; sources carry trust_tier and license_class; unique index on (content_hash, raw_blob_id) prevents duplicates (pinned by integration test for the realistic non-conflict case where they differ); parent_document_id supports threaded sources with traversal helpers; deleted_at column present for retention; raw blobs are read-only (ObjectStore has no update/delete; content-addressed). Verified with services/evidence npm test (50 pass: unit + Docker-gated integration via bootstrapMinio + bootstrapDatabase) and db npm test (34 pass). Three rounds of code review (per-bead × 2 + cross-cutting integration) caught and resolved: contract-divergence between document-repo and object-store, BFS-vs-DFS terminology mismatch, parent_document_id silent-keep on conflict, isNotFoundError too-broad 404 fallback (R2 edge case), and FIFO t.after ordering. Branch feat/fra-gfq-source-doc-repos head 8056278.

title: P3.2 Document ingestion & reader extraction
description:
# P3.2 — Document ingestion & reader extraction

## What

The reader pipeline: acquisition from each source class (SEC filings, issuer press, earnings transcripts, news articles, research notes, X/tweets, Reddit posts/threads, user uploads), canonicalization, parsing, entity-linking, and extraction of claims / candidate-facts / events / impacts / sentiment via the reader-audience tools (`extract_mentions`, `extract_claims`, `extract_candidate_facts`, `extract_events`, `classify_sentiment`).

## Why

The reader boundary (I4) is the prompt-injection containment frontier. If raw untrusted text ever reaches the analyst, a malicious document can steer downstream tool calls. Keeping all raw-text handling inside reader-audience tools means analyst-audience code paths are statically incapable of being poisoned.

## Contract / acceptance

- Reader-only tools: `search_raw_documents`, `fetch_raw_document`, `extract_mentions`, `extract_claims`, `extract_candidate_facts`, `extract_events`, `classify_sentiment` (per `finance_research_tool_registry.json`).
- These tools are the ONLY code paths that may access raw blob bytes.
- Analyst-audience tools receive structured outputs: claims, events, candidate facts, authoritative facts, evidence bundles, entity impacts, claim clusters.
- Content-hash dedupe at ingest; `content_hash` identical → one `documents` row.
- Each ingestion writes: `documents` row, `mentions`, `claims` + `claim_arguments` + `entity_impacts`, `claim_evidence`, `events` + `event_subjects`, candidate facts.
- Per-source-class promotion rules are applied (see P3.5).
- Failed extractions below a confidence threshold queue into a reviewer queue (P6.3) rather than silently auto-promoting.
- Ingestion is idempotent: replaying the same source material does not duplicate rows.

## Depends on

- P3.1 (document/source models + object storage)
- PX.2 (reader-audience tool runtime — enforces the boundary)

## Relationship to P2.4 (snapshot assembler)

**P3.2 is NOT blocked by P2.4.** Extracted evidence (claims, facts, events) lands in the evidence plane independently. P2.4 *consumes* these records at snapshot-seal time — it does not gate extraction. The two can be built in parallel once P3.1 + PX.2 exist.

## Downstream consumers

- P3.3, P3.4, P3.5 (downstream extraction/clustering/promotion)
- P4.6 (specialized social/news blocks read extracted claims)
- P5.2 (agent runs rerun ingestion over deltas)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Prompt-injection containment: tests must verify that no reader-extracted text can steer analyst behavior.
- SEC EDGAR rate-limit rules apply: respect User-Agent policy and request rate.
- Reddit/X content may have restrictive licensing — store metadata + canonical URL, not always the full body.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/evidence/ingest/sec/*`
- `services/evidence/ingest/news/*`
- `services/evidence/ingest/social/*`
- `services/evidence/ingest/upload/*`
- `services/evidence/reader/*` (extraction tools)

title: P2.3 Block registry versioning & initial block catalog
description:
# P2.3 — Block registry versioning & initial block catalog

## What

The `BlockRegistry`: a typed registry keyed by block `kind` with a JSON-schema validator wired to `spec/finance_research_block_schema.json` and a renderer per block kind. Assistant output is **always** `Block[]`, not markdown and not tool calls. Chat, Analyze, and agent-produced findings render through the same registry.

## Why

A shared registry is how "generative UI" stays coherent: rich_text in chat, a Finding card on Home, and an Analyze memo section all use the same React component instance with the same data_ref/snapshot_id contract. Without the registry, each surface invents its own renderer and the snapshot contract fragments.

## Contract / acceptance

- Every block validates against `spec/finance_research_block_schema.json`.
- BaseBlock fields are universal: `id`, `kind`, `snapshot_id`, `data_ref`, `source_refs[]`, `as_of`, optional `title`, `disclosure_tier`, `interactive`.
- Canonical schema field is `data_ref` (NOT `dataRef` or `queryRef`).
- Block kinds in the initial catalog: `rich_text`, `section`, `metric_row`, `table`, `line_chart`, `revenue_bars`, `perf_comparison`, `segment_donut`, `segment_trajectory`, `metrics_comparison`, `analyst_consensus`, `price_target_range`, `eps_surprise`, `filings_list`, `news_cluster`, `finding_card`, `sentiment_trend`, `mention_volume`, `sources`, `disclosure`.
- `RichText` binds facts/claims/events by reference: segments are `{type: "text", text}` or `{type: "ref", ref_kind, ref_id, format?}`.
- `Section` is first-class and holds child blocks.
- `Sources` is required whenever external evidence was used.
- `Disclosure` may be injected by orchestration.
- Registry versioning: adding a block kind is a backward-compatible change; renaming/removing requires a migration.
- Chat, Analyze, and findings render via the same registry keyed by `kind`.

## Depends on

- P1.1b (stable series query identity + in-snapshot cache rules — so interactive blocks know which transforms are legal)
- P2.4 (snapshot rules — registry needs to know what's sealed)

## Downstream consumers

- PX.3 (frontend renderer uses the registry)
- P4.2 (Analyze memos render via this)
- P4.4 (Home feed renders finding_card via this)
- P5.3 (agent findings render summary_blocks via this)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Do NOT let the model emit HTML or markdown. Any "markdown-ish" block is rendered client-side from typed rich_text segments.
- RichText ref resolution is client-side: the renderer looks up fact/claim/event ids in the snapshot manifest.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `app/blocks/Registry.ts`
- `app/blocks/<Kind>.tsx` (one per kind)
- `services/chat/BlockValidator.ts` (JSON-schema wire)
close_reason: Accepted P2.3 after verifying all block-registry child beads are closed and web gates pass: schema validator, canonical data_ref naming, 20 block renderer catalog, RichText ref resolver, fixture coverage, tests, typecheck, and lint.

title: P2.1 Thread coordinator & SSE transport
description:
# P2.1 — Thread coordinator & SSE transport

## What

Stateful per-thread coordinator (Durable-Object-style on Cloudflare, or a process-local actor elsewhere) plus SSE transport for streaming assistant responses. Threads are session-scoped; chat is a primary workspace inside the persistent shell. One coordinator per `thread_id` serializes turn state, tool calls, snapshot staging, and SSE event ordering.

## Why

Serialized per-thread coordination prevents race conditions when tool calls complete out of order, when a user sends a second message mid-stream, or when a verifier rejection must be surfaced alongside a partially-streamed response. Durable Objects are a strong fit because each object is globally addressable, single-threaded, and has strongly-consistent colocated storage.

## Contract / acceptance

- One coordinator per `thread_id`; serializes turn execution.
- SSE event schema: `turn.started`, `tool.started`, `tool.completed`, `snapshot.staged`, `snapshot.sealed`, `block.began`, `block.delta`, `block.completed`, `turn.completed`, `turn.error`.
- Events carry `turn_id`, `block_id` (where relevant), and monotonically increasing sequence numbers.
- Reconnection resumes from the last delivered sequence.
- Chat lives inside the persistent shell (P0.1); the coordinator is session-scoped.
- The coordinator persists `chat_threads.latest_snapshot_id` after sealing.
- `chat_messages` are written after the snapshot is sealed — never before.

## Depends on

- P0.1 (shell — Chat is a primary workspace inside)
- P0.6 (SSE route stub — replaces with real wiring here)
- P2.4 (snapshot sealing — writes happen after sealing)

## Downstream consumers

- P2.3 (block registry — blocks stream via `block.began/delta/completed`)
- PX.3 (frontend renderer — consumes SSE and drives UI updates)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Cloudflare Durable Objects have a single-thread execution model — use it deliberately for serialization but don't starve concurrent turns for different threads.
- Don't write `chat_messages` until `snapshot.sealed` — persisting before verifier approval means an invariant-violating message becomes history.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/chat/ThreadCoordinator.ts` (Durable Object or actor equivalent)
- `services/chat/SSE.ts`
- `/v1/chat/threads/*` handlers
close_reason: All five children closed; acceptance contract met: per-thread coordinator (fra-eom), SSE event schema + monotonic seq (fra-u9l), Last-Event-ID reconnect (fra-cty), chat_messages persisted only after snapshot sealing (fra-d7t), /v1/chat/threads CRUD (fra-2fu.1.1).

title: P2.2 Pre-resolve router & budget policy
description:
# P2.2 — Pre-resolve router & budget policy

## What

A deterministic orchestration stage that runs *before* the analyst tool loop: extracts subject candidates, resolves the period, loads the thread summary and prior snapshot, detects trivial fast paths, enforces auth + rate limits, selects the tool bundle, and sets the turn's cost budget. The analyst never sees raw user text without first going through this stage.

## Why

Deterministic pre-resolve consumes resolver envelopes rather than asking the model to silently choose among ambiguous identity candidates. It also keeps token costs bounded: distinguishing lightweight quote reads from heavier historical-bar/fundamentals/evidence reads lets the budget policy pick the right cost class without guessing from provider names or ticker text.

## Contract / acceptance

- Inputs: user message, thread summary, prior snapshot manifest (if any), auth context.
- Stages: subject extraction → period resolution → fast-path detection → auth/rate gate → bundle selection → budget setting.
- Subject extraction uses P0.3 resolver; ambiguity is preserved (not silently resolved).
- Bundle selection: the **system** chooses the bundle; the **model** chooses tools within the bundle.
- Bundles (from `finance_research_tool_registry.json`): `quote_lookup`, `single_subject_analysis`, `peer_comparison`, `theme_research`, `segment_deep_dive`, `document_research`, `filing_research`, `screener`, `agent_management`, `alert_management`, `analyze_template_run`.
- Budget policy has per-cost-class caps (low/medium/high tool calls) and a global per-turn cap.
- Prompt-cache ordering is respected: the prefix is `tools → system → bundle policy → response schema → few-shots → thread summary → resolved subjects/period`; volatile user turn is last.

## Depends on

- P0.3 (resolver)
- P0.3b (search-to-subject flow)
- P1.1 (quote cost class)
- P1.2 (fundamentals cost class)
- PX.2 (tool runtime + registry)

## Downstream consumers

- P2.1 (thread coordinator invokes this before the analyst loop)
- P5.2 (agent runs reuse the same bundle-selection discipline)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Never let raw user text bypass this stage into tool calls — prompt-injection containment starts here.
- Prompt-cache TTL is 5 minutes (per Anthropic caching docs). Building the cacheable prefix correctly materially affects cost.
- Fast-path detection should produce a deterministic response (e.g., a quote-only turn) without invoking the analyst at all.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/chat/PreResolve.ts`
- `services/chat/BundleSelector.ts`
- `services/chat/BudgetPolicy.ts`
close_reason: Closed

title: P1.5b Portfolio & watchlist first surface behaviors
description:
# P1.5b — Portfolio & watchlist first surface behaviors

## What

The first-render surface behaviors for manual watchlists and portfolio-held subjects. Reuses the thin quote-on-read row hydration from P0.4 for both, layering held-state + quantity + cost-basis context on top of the shared quote row skeleton for portfolio-held rows.

## Why

Inventing separate quote-fetch behavior for held subjects creates a second identity model and breaks the shared-subject contract. Saving a subject (watchlist) and holding a subject (portfolio) must remain separate concepts even though their rows share the same hydration skeleton.

## Contract / acceptance

- Manual watchlist + portfolio-held surfaces reuse the same thin quote-on-read row hydration as P0.4.
- Portfolio-held rows layer held-state, quantity, and optional cost-basis context on top of the shared quote row skeleton.
- First surface stays intentionally light: no portfolio analytics dashboard, brokerage position card, or private quote model distinct from symbol entry.
- If a subject is both watchlisted and held, the surface keeps both states visible — does NOT collapse one concept into the other.
- Selecting a row from either a watchlist or portfolio-held surface enters the same symbol-detail route keyed by canonical subject identity.
- Entered subject views may show lightweight saved-state and held-state context plus adjacent actions — context augments existing quote snapshot + subject modules, NOT a portfolio-specific subject shell.
- Watchlist and holdings state remain user-owned overlay context on top of shared subject + market-data contracts.

## Depends on

- P1.5 (holdings model + overlay inputs)
- P0.4b (manual watchlist baseline)

## Downstream consumers

- P4.7 (dynamic watchlists + portfolio overlays — reuse shared row and subject-view augmentation)
- P6.4 (export/share policy — user-owned state stays distinct from canonical content for export gating)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Collapsing watchlist + held states into one flag loses the distinction the videos treat as meaningful.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `app/watchlists/*` (shared row component)
- `app/portfolio/HeldRows.tsx`
close_reason: P1.5b delivered. Watchlist + portfolio-held surfaces share one quote-row skeleton (cw0.10.1) — both routes flow through <QuoteRow> + the pure quoteRowView projection so the same subject renders identical ticker / venue / price / percent move on either surface; held rows layer quantity + cost-basis on the shared skeleton via the trailing slot. Both-states visibility (cw0.10.2) reports watchlisted and held independently — a subject that is both shows two distinct badges; pure isSubjectWatchlisted() + useSubjectHeld() are derived orthogonally and never collapse into a 'saved' indicator. Watchlist membership lifts into a context provider in WorkspaceShell so the rail and the subject-detail header read from one source. First surface stays intentionally light: no analytics dashboard, no portfolio-specific subject shell.

title: P1.4b Screener surface flow & saved-screen handoff
description:
# P1.4b — Screener surface flow & saved-screen handoff

## What

The screener UI workspace and the session-gated saved-screen lifecycle. Screener is a primary workspace for building, refining, and viewing one active screen definition plus its current result set inside the persistent shell.

## Why

Splitting query editing, results, and saved screens into separate surfaces would force the user to context-switch for every refinement. Unifying them inside one screener workspace matches the videos' flow.

## Contract / acceptance

- Screener keeps query controls and result rows in one workspace flow. No separate "query editor" or "results viewer" primary surfaces.
- Public screener browsing may execute and refine unsaved screens without session (uses the public-route contract).
- Saving a screen / opening user-owned saved screen / mutating saved-screen metadata requires a session and uses the inline auth interrupt from P0.1.
- Saving persists the replayable screen definition + user-owned screen record + ordering semantics — NOT a frozen cache of row payloads.
- Reopening a saved screen restores the workspace with saved-screen definition as active query context; rehydrates results through the screener service (no replaying stale table rows from client storage).
- Selecting a result row hands off canonical subject identity into the existing symbol-detail entry flow.
- Handoffs into later list or theme workflows carry explicit `screen` source reference so downstream derivation can explain where a universe came from.
- This bead does NOT define dynamic watchlist derivation, theme membership inference, or bulk portfolio-overlay behavior (those belong to P4.7).

## Depends on

- P1.4 (query model)
- P0.1 (auth interrupt)
- P0.4 (symbol-entry flow — row selection)

## Downstream consumers

- P4.1 (themes — screen-to-theme handoff)
- P4.7 (dynamic watchlists — saved screen as replayable source)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Row selection is handoff of `SubjectRef`, not embed of a workspace. Don't render a full symbol workspace inline.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `app/screener/ScreenerWorkspace.tsx`
- `app/screener/SavedScreens.tsx`
close_reason: P1.4b delivered. Screener workspace (cw0.8.1) keeps query controls + result rows in one /screener surface — refining a filter reissues POST /v1/screener/search and re-renders rows in place; URL never leaves /screener. Row click hands off canonical SubjectRef to symbol-detail. Saved-screen CRUD (cw0.8.2) gates Save / Open / Delete through the inline auth interrupt: SaveScreen protected-action variant carries the full ScreenerQuery envelope through sessionStorage, surviving sign-in. Open re-hydrates results through the screener service, not from a frozen client cache.
